content.text: Arc Kubernetes cluster extensions install capabilities onto connected clusters via Helm charts managed as ARM resources. Each extension is a Microsoft.KubernetesConfiguration/extensions ARM resource — creating one triggers the Arc agent to download the Helm chart from Azure Container Registry and install it to the cluster. Extensions have two scopes: cluster-scoped (one instance per cluster, used for operators like Gatekeeper, Flux, and Defender) and namespace-scoped (multiple instances per cluster, used for per-team tools like Dapr). Extension state is reported back to ARM via the resource-sync-agent — installState: Installed / Pending / Failed. Most extensions support auto-upgrade within minor versions; pin to a specific version for extensions that have breaking changes between minor releases.

attrs.alt: Arc K8s cluster extensions: lifecycle model, cluster vs namespace scope, and available extension types

content.text: Extension type IDs are lowercase and use dot notation (microsoft.flux, microsoft.policyinsights). The --name flag is the ARM resource name for your deployment — it can differ from the extension type, but conventionally matches for clarity.
Deploy App Service to on-prem K8s
MQTT broker, data pipelines at edge

Install, inspect, update, and delete extensions using 'az k8s-extension' commands. Pass Helm chart values via --configuration-settings (plaintext, shows in ARM metadata) or --configuration-protected-settings (encrypted, not visible in ARM after creation — use for secrets like workspace IDs or connection strings). Update configuration settings with 'az k8s-extension update' — this performs a Helm upgrade on the cluster. Check the extension's pods and namespace to verify install health; 'kubectl describe pod' and 'kubectl logs --previous' are the first debugging tools for failed extensions.

# Arc K8s extension fundamentals: create, manage, inspect

# Extension create pattern
az k8s-extension create \
  --name '<extension-name>' \
  --extension-type '<extension-type-id>' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name '<cluster-name>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --scope cluster \
  --release-namespace '<target-ns>' \
  --auto-upgrade-minor-version true \
  --version '1.0.0' \
  --configuration-settings key=value \
  --configuration-protected-settings key=secret

# List extensions on a cluster
az k8s-extension list \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Type:extensionType, State:installState, Version:version, AutoUpgrade:autoUpgradeMinorVersion}' \
  -o table

# Show extension details including Helm chart config
az k8s-extension show \
  --name 'azuremonitor-containers' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '{State:installState, Version:version, Config:configurationSettings}' \
  -o json

# Update extension configuration settings (Helm values)
az k8s-extension update \
  --name 'azuremonitor-containers' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --configuration-settings \
    amalogs.useAADAuth=true \
    logAnalyticsWorkspaceResourceID='/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws>'

# Check extension pods in the cluster
kubectl get pods -n azuremonitor-containers
kubectl get pods -n gatekeeper-system
kubectl get pods -n flux-system

# Describe a failing extension pod for troubleshooting
kubectl describe pod -n <ext-namespace> <failing-pod-name>
kubectl logs -n <ext-namespace> <failing-pod-name> --previous
Dapr and Open Service Mesh Extensions

Dapr (Distributed Application Runtime) and OSM (Open Service Mesh) are application platform extensions that enable microservices patterns on Arc K8s. Dapr installs a system namespace and injects a daprd sidecar into pods with the dapr.io/enabled annotation — enabling pub/sub (Azure Service Bus, Event Hubs), service-to-service invocation with retries and circuit breaking, and secrets from Azure Key Vault without SDK changes. OSM installs Envoy sidecar proxies into annotated namespaces, enforcing mTLS between pods and traffic policy via SMI (Service Mesh Interface) resources. Do not run Dapr and OSM simultaneously without careful namespace isolation — both inject sidecars and can interfere.

# Dapr and OSM (Open Service Mesh) extensions

# --- Dapr: distributed application runtime ---
# Enables pub/sub, service-to-service invocation, state management, secrets
# without code changes via sidecar injection

# 1. Install Dapr extension
az k8s-extension create \
  --name dapr \
  --extension-type microsoft.dapr \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster \
  --configuration-settings \
    global.ha.enabled=true \
    dapr_operator.replicaCount=2

# Verify Dapr system pods
kubectl get pods -n dapr-system

# 2. Enable Dapr sidecar injection on a namespace
kubectl label namespace production dapr.io/enabled=true

# 3. Deploy an app with Dapr annotations (Dapr injects daprd sidecar automatically)
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: myapp
      annotations:
        dapr.io/enabled: 'true'
        dapr.io/app-id: 'myapp'
        dapr.io/app-port: '8080'
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
EOF

# --- OSM: Open Service Mesh ---
# mTLS between pods, traffic policy, observability for service-to-service

# 1. Install OSM extension
az k8s-extension create \
  --name osm \
  --extension-type microsoft.openservicemesh \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# 2. Enable OSM sidecar injection on a namespace
kubectl label namespace production openservicemesh.io/monitored-by=osm
osm namespace add production

# 3. Create an HTTPRouteGroup to allow traffic between services
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: specs.smi-spec.io/v1alpha4
kind: HTTPRouteGroup
metadata:
  name: api-routes
  namespace: production
spec:
  matches:
  - name: api
    pathRegex: /api/.*
    methods:
    - GET
    - POST
EOF

# Verify mTLS between pods (all traffic encrypted by OSM Envoy sidecars)
osm verify connectivity \
  --from-pod production/myapp-<pod-id> \
  --to-service production/backend-svc

The Defender for Containers extension (microsoft.azuredefender.kubernetes) ships telemetry to Microsoft Defender for Cloud and enables runtime threat detection on Arc K8s clusters. Alerts include: privileged container execution, sensitive file access (/etc/shadow, /etc/passwd), command execution into a pod via kubectl exec, cryptomining processes, reverse shell connections, and lateral movement attempts. Hardening recommendations (read-only root filesystem, non-root user, no privilege escalation, drop all Linux capabilities) are surfaced in Defender for Cloud and can be enforced via the Azure Policy Gatekeeper extension simultaneously.

# Defender for Containers on Arc K8s: threat detection and hardening

# 1. Install Defender extension (requires Defender for Containers plan on subscription)
az k8s-extension create \
  --name microsoft-defender \
  --extension-type microsoft.azuredefender.kubernetes \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster \
  --configuration-settings \
    logAnalyticsWorkspaceResourceID='/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws>'

# Verify Defender pods are running
kubectl get pods -n microsoft-defender

# 2. Check Defender for Containers coverage in Defender for Cloud
az security assessment list \
  --query "[?contains(name, 'arc-k8s')]" \
  -o table

# 3. View security alerts for the Arc K8s cluster
az security alert list \
  --query "[?contains(associatedResource, 'connectedClusters')]" \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Severity:severity, Status:status, Description:description}' \
  -o table

# 4. Simulate a Defender alert (for testing — exec into pod = suspicious activity)
# In a test cluster only:
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n default -- /bin/bash
# cat /etc/shadow  (triggers 'Sensitive files access' alert within 5 min)

# 5. Hardening recommendations from Defender
#    'Kubernetes workloads should use read only root file system'
#    'Containers should not run as root'
#    'Privileged containers should be avoided'
# Apply security context to remediate:
cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: hardened-app
  namespace: default
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: hardened-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: hardened-app
    spec:
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 2000
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest
        securityContext:
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          capabilities:
            drop: [ALL]
EOF
Installing the same extension twice with different names
Two Helm releases in same namespace conflict; second install fails
Each extension type can only be installed once per cluster; delete old before reinstalling
--version pins the chart version; auto-upgrade is ignored
Use one or the other — version pin OR auto-upgrade, not both
Installing App Service extension without creating a Custom Location first
App Service plan create fails — no Custom Location to target
Create Custom Location referencing the extension AFTER install; then create App Service plan
Not granting workspace write permissions to the Container Insights MSI
AMA ships data but Log Analytics rejects it; no data appears
Grant Log Analytics Contributor to the extension MSI on the workspace resource
Deleting an extension without removing dependent resources first
Custom Locations, App Service plans referencing the extension become broken
Delete App Service plans and Custom Locations before deleting the extension
Installing Dapr and OSM together without traffic policy
Both inject sidecars; mutual interference causes connection resets
Disable OSM injection on Dapr-annotated namespaces, or use only one service mesh

Arc K8s cluster extensions install capabilities as ARM-managed Helm charts. Key extensions: microsoft.flux (GitOps), microsoft.azuremonitor.containers (Container Insights), microsoft.policyinsights (Gatekeeper OPA), microsoft.azuredefender.kubernetes (Defender for Containers), microsoft.dapr (Dapr sidecar), microsoft.openservicemesh (OSM mTLS), and microsoft.app.environment (App Service on Arc, requires Custom Location). Use --configuration-settings for Helm values and --configuration-protected-settings for secrets. Most extensions support auto-upgrade within minor versions. Do not run Dapr and OSM on the same namespaces. Delete App Service plans and Custom Locations before deleting the App Service extension. Extension install failures are diagnosed via kubectl describe/logs on the extension namespace pods.
